story:
I am, most assuredly, not a prince. If you kiss me, I will remain exactly what I am, which is gloriously, incontrovertibly frog. I have not been alive for very long, but I know certain things to be true, such as:
1) I was born in a small creek that winds through untamed woods until it meets a river some “miles” away (whatever those are).
2) I am excellent at sounding knowledgeable.
3) I am even better at making lists.
4) I am not in that creek anymore.
5) I am in water, with a stone nearby and grass and flowers strewn about. It is cozy.
6) It is also a jar, which makes it a jail.
7) I must escape.

The culprit is a tall, loud creature called Maggie. Her hair clatters with big beads; her neck wears a noisy rainbow of insect talismans. She kidnapped me from my favorite lilypad and insists on calling me “Prince Charming,” which is not my name. I named myself Frog. It captures my froginess, my frogativity, my froglination, and, of course, my frogutory frogutativeness. “Prince Charming” says nothing about me. Maggie also keeps trying to kiss me on the head. I have, to my credit, avoided her smooches so far.

She often leans close to my glass cell and studies me for long, still minutes. It is flattering—after all, I am the most frog out of anyone I know—but she does not grasp my frogliniacy. She talks about turning me into a prince, which is frankly insulting. If I could speak in a language she understood, I would tell her so. Alas, she is not yet advanced enough to understand my most glorious, frogulatious speech.

Shh. She is coming.

She flings open the door and jingles straight to my jar. “Hello, little Prince!” (Still not my title.) Then, instead of kissing me, she spills her day at me like sunlight over water: “Jenna B. was mean to me again. She pushed me on the playground. She used to be my best friend, but now she sits with Jenna S., and I got in trouble for making Jenna S. cry, even though Jenna S. is mean.” I have never heard of these Jennas, but Maggie is, for all her flaws, a rapturous storyteller, and my reed-thin heart listens.

She keeps going—about lost lunches and empty after-school hours, about how friends can live right down the street and still feel far away. Her lower lip trembles. A fat tear gathers. I cannot speak in a way she will hear, but inside my head the words aren’t hard: Sometimes people need space to figure themselves out. Maybe Jenna B. is trying new things. Maybe, by resisting that, you pushed her away. Have you tried apologizing to Jenna S. and being friends with both?

“You’re right, Mr. Prince,” she says suddenly, and smiles. Did she hear me? Perhaps she heard only what she needed. Then she calls me a frog—her frog—and a friend. That, I admit, fits. Maybe I am a prince to her. Maybe I can be both.

“I love you, Prince Charming,” she whispers, and a tear escapes. I pause, then wish her the best in the only language I have. In my heart, I answer: I love you too.

The next day, Maggie, Jenna B., the once-mean-now-maybe-not Jenna S., and Momma ride in a noble steed named Honda to the small creek of my birth. They set me down among grass and flowers. “Goodbye, Prince Charming!” they call, bright as bells. My frogituous heart swells.

Goodbye, Maggie. Goodbye, Jennas. Goodbye, Momma. Goodbye, Honda.

It was a pleasure knowing you.
